explanation: This LTL property states that globally, if the state is NOMINAL and the autopilot is not requesting support (not request), then eventually the state will transition to TRANSITION. The property effectively captures the requirement that the sensor should change states from NOMINAL to TRANSITION when the autopilot is not requesting support.

The LTL property states that it is always the case that when the autopilot is not requesting support (not request) and the state is NOMINAL, the next state is TRANSITION. The property uses a G (Globally) operator over an implication. The implication's antecedent is 'not request and state_is_NOMINAL', and its consequent is the next state is TRANSITION. N_DURATION is not used here, the desired effect is immediate change from NOMINAL to TRANSITION after the antecedent is true.

The property states that eventually the state will change from NOMINAL to TRANSITION if the autopilot is not requesting support. The LTL formula ensures that if the system is in the NOMINAL state and then at some point the autopilot does not request support, then eventually the state becomes TRANSITION.  Template ID 129, is being used as it has a similar structure, allowing for the eventual fulfillment of a condition (state change) under another condition (not request).  bool_exp1 represents the condition "state_is_NOMINAL", bool_exp3 represents the condition "state_is_TRANSITION", the boolean expression "!request" is used to represent the condition of the autopilot not requesting support, and the bool_exp2 isn't used in the specified templates.
chosen_LTL_template: ((G ((! (bool_exp1 & (X (! bool_exp1)))) | (X ((! ((! bool_exp1) & (X bool_exp1))) U bool_exp3)))) & ((! bool_exp1) -> ((! ((! bool_exp1) & (X bool_exp1))) U bool_exp3)))

This captures the prompt's requirement that the sensor changes state from NOMINAL to TRANSITION when the autopilot is not requesting support.

This LTL property states that the state should always transition from NOMINAL to TRANSITION when the autopilot is not requesting support. This is captured by the G (Globally) operator ensuring the implication holds true at every point in time. The implication itself is represented as (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3). bool_exp2 is the autopilot not requesting support. bool_exp3 is state transitions from NOMINAL to TRANSITION.

The LTL property states that globally, if the autopilot is not requesting support (not request), then it will eventually transition the sensor state from NOMINAL to TRANSITION. The LTL property ensures that globally (G), if the autopilot is not requesting support (not request), then eventually (F[0,N_DURATION]) the state becomes TRANSITION. The property also states that the state will start in a NOMINAL state, if it's not in the TRANSITION state or FAULT state.

The LTL property states that globally, if the state is NOMINAL and the autopilot is not requesting support (not request), then it will eventually transition to TRANSITION. The property ensures the desired state change under the specified condition.

The LTL property states that globally, if the sensor is in the NOMINAL state and the autopilot is not requesting support (!request), then eventually, within a certain time frame, the sensor state will transition to TRANSITION. Specifically, it checks for a state change after the condition of not requesting support is met. I used template 199, because the phrase 'shall change states from ... to ... when' indicates a state change, and the 'when' clause suggests a conditional relationship. The condition given is on autopilot state changes. The formula uses a 'V' operator (unless) to guarantee the transition within a time frame. Finally, this is an unless property because if support is always requested, it should never change to the other state.
chosen_LTL_template: ((((! bool_exp1) & (X bool_exp1)) V (bool_exp2 -> ((! ((! bool_exp1) & (X bool_exp1))) U bool_exp3))) | bool_exp1)
